questions.question: Match each memory stage to its definition:
categories: Experiences alter the nervous system, creating memory traces

correct_mapping.Encoding: Experiences alter the nervous system, creating memory traces

questions.explanation: The three stages of memory: encoding transforms experiences into neural traces (synaptic changes), storage retains them over time through consolidation processes, and retrieval accesses stored traces using external or internal cues.

questions.question: Match each memory type to its description:
categories: Overlap of episodic + semantic, creates sense of self
Implicit skills and habits, spared in amnesia

correct_mapping.Autobiographical memory: Overlap of episodic + semantic, creates sense of self
correct_mapping.Nondeclarative memory: Implicit skills and habits, spared in amnesia

questions.explanation: Semantic = decontextualised facts, episodic = contextual events, autobiographical = personal narrative combining both, nondeclarative = implicit skills/habits that don't require conscious recollection and are spared in MTL amnesia.

questions.question: Match each consolidation theory to its claim about remote episodic memories:
categories: Remote memories become hippocampus-independent via direct cortical connections
Episodic memories ALWAYS need the hippocampus, older = more traces = more resistant

correct_mapping.Standard Consolidation Theory: Remote memories become hippocampus-independent via direct cortical connections
correct_mapping.Multiple Trace Theory: Episodic memories ALWAYS need the hippocampus, older = more traces = more resistant

questions.explanation: Standard theory says remote memories eventually form direct cortical connections and no longer need the hippocampus. MTT says episodic memories always require hippocampal traces, but older memories have more traces making them more resistant to partial hippocampal damage.

questions.question: Match each hippocampal theory to its focus:
categories: Hippocampus as the seat of episodic memory

correct_mapping.Episodic Memory Theory (Tulving): Hippocampus as the seat of episodic memory

questions.explanation: Three theories: Cognitive Map (right HC for spatial representation), Relational Memory (left HC for binding/associating elements), and Tulving's Episodic Memory Theory (hippocampus is essential for episodic memory encoding and retrieval).

questions.question: Match each consolidation type to its characteristics:
categories: Fast, occurs at the synapse level
Slow, transfers from MTL to distributed cortex

correct_mapping.Synaptic consolidation: Fast, occurs at the synapse level
correct_mapping.System consolidation: Slow, transfers from MTL to distributed cortex

questions.explanation: Synaptic consolidation is fast and local (strengthening connections at individual synapses). System consolidation is slow and large-scale (gradually transferring memory dependence from MTL to distributed cortical networks).

questions.question: Match each PPC region to its role in memory:
questions.explanation: Dorsal PPC supports strategic, goal-directed memory search (top-down attention), while ventral PPC responds to salient or unexpected information (bottom-up attention). Ventral parietal encoding activation actually predicts subsequent forgetting.

questions.question: Match each Baddeley component to what it handles:
questions.explanation: Each working memory component has a distinct role: phonological loop for verbal/acoustic info, visuospatial sketchpad for spatial/visual info, episodic buffer for integration, and central executive for attentional control.

questions.question: Match each brain structure to its role in the neurological memory model:
questions.explanation: The hippocampus indexes distributed cortical traces, cortical regions store the actual content (visual, auditory, etc.), the amygdala enhances emotional memory consolidation, and the PFC supports both encoding and retrieval executive processes.

questions.question: Match each lesion type to its memory consequence:
categories: Mild memory deficits (distributed traces only partially lost)
Severe amnesia (index destroyed, can't link traces)

correct_mapping.Small cortical lesion: Mild memory deficits (distributed traces only partially lost)
correct_mapping.Bilateral MTL lesion: Severe amnesia (index destroyed, can't link traces)

questions.explanation: Small cortical lesions cause mild deficits because traces are distributed. Bilateral MTL lesions cause severe amnesia because the hippocampal index is destroyed. Amygdala damage impairs the emotional modulation of memory consolidation.

questions.question: Match each PFC encoding/retrieval characteristic:
categories: Multiple PFC subregions — less regionally specific

correct_mapping.PFC retrieval role: Multiple PFC subregions — less regionally specific

questions.explanation: PFC encoding is relatively specific to the inferior frontal gyrus. PFC retrieval involves multiple subregions and is less regionally specific, reflecting the broader executive demands of memory search and evaluation during retrieval.
